items.q: Does the program counter hold the current instruction?
items.correct: No - the program counter holds the ADDRESS of the next instruction; the current instruction itself sits in the current instruction register after being fetched.
items.misconception: Yes - the program counter stores the instruction being executed.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the program counter counts how many programs are installed.
items.why: Both registers are involved in every cycle, so they blur together. Separate the address (PC, a pointer into memory) from the content (CIR, the fetched instruction).

items.q: Does a higher clock speed always mean a faster computer?
items.correct: No - performance also depends on cores, cache size, memory speed and how much work each cycle achieves, so a lower-clocked CPU can outperform a higher-clocked one.
items.misconception: Yes - clock speed is the only factor that determines performance.
items.random_wrong: Yes - doubling the GHz exactly doubles every program's speed.
items.why: Clock speed is the most advertised number, so it dominates intuition. It only counts cycles per second, saying nothing about work per cycle or memory bottlenecks.

items.q: In the von Neumann architecture, are instructions and data stored in separate memories?
items.correct: No - they share one main memory and one set of buses; the Harvard architecture is the design that separates instruction and data memory.
items.misconception: Yes - von Neumann machines keep programs and data physically apart.
items.random_wrong: Yes - instructions are stored only inside the ALU.
items.why: The two named architectures get swapped. The shared memory and bus of von Neumann is what creates its famous bottleneck, which Harvard designs avoid.

items.q: Which register holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched?
items.why: Correct answer: Program counter. “Memory buffer register” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: During the fetch stage, the address held in the program counter is copied to the:
items.why: It's Memory address register. “Accumulator” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which bus carries memory addresses from the processor to main memory?
items.why: Correct answer: Address bus. “Data bus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A defining feature of the von Neumann architecture is that:
items.correct: Instructions and data are stored in the same memory and share the same buses
items.misconception: Instructions and data are kept in physically separate memories
items.random_wrong: No registers are used by the processor
items.why: It's Instructions and data are stored in the same memory and share the same buses. “Instructions and data are kept in physically separate memories” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A CPU completes one instruction per clock cycle and runs at 2 GHz. How many billion instructions does it complete per second?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 billion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
